text: Following the lysis of peritoneal adhesions, the surgeon identifies and dissects the ureters in their pelvic portions. Resection margins are visibly sent for pathological examination. The pouch of Douglas is opened, and the posterior aspect of the bladder is dissected, with careful technique employed to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon uses low-energy bipolar cautery to dissect the seminal vesicles free, taking care to avoid damage to the neurovascular bundles. The omvius fascia is sectioned, and the posterior plane is developed until the prostatic apex is visually identified.

The surgeon dissects the lateral walls of the bladder by developing the paravesical spaces. The vasa deferentia are identified and sectioned, while the endopelvic fascia is carefully identified and preserved.

The surgeon identifies, dissects, and sections the bladder pedicles in an undergrade fashion, aligning the dissection plane with the previously identified posterior plane during prostatic dissection. The lateral pelvic lymph nodes are prepared for removal following the completion of the extirpative phase.

The surgeon lifts the bladder and uses low-energy bipolar cautery to preserve the neurovascular bundles through interfacial dissection. The dissection proceeds in an antegrade fashion until reaching the puboprostatic ligaments. The surgical field shows occasional bleeding due to perforating vessels from the neurovascular bundles to the prostate. Robotic arms maintain general traction to help identify the proper dissection plane.

The surgeon frees the anterior surface of the bladder up to the puboprostatic ligaments, preserving these ligaments. The DBC or Santorini plexus is identified and sectioned, followed by hemostasis achieved through a running suture.

The surgeon dissects and sections the urethra at the level of the prostatic apex. The Foley catheter provides traction on the posterior surface of the urethra and the fibers of the recto-urethralis muscle. The surgical specimen is then placed into an endobag.

The surgeon performs the urethra neovascular anastomosis approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The bowel is anchored to the perirethral tissue, and an incision is made to define the anastomotic rim. A barbed suture is used to start the anastomosis, and a new catheter is inserted before closing the suture.
The surgeon uses a periscopic stapler to isolate a segment of bowel, visually separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to remove the staple lines. A lateral-lateral anastomosis in the bowel is then performed using a laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon performs tubularization of the bowel using monopolar cautery on the anti-mesenteric aspects. Gentle retraction is applied using the surgical arms to align the two newly identified posterior segments.
The surgeon performs a posterior continuous suture on the bowel, followed by de-tubularization of the remaining ileal segment. A second posterior continuous suture is applied to secure the tissue.

The surgeon performs an anterior continuous suture to close the neobladder. Ureteral catheters are visibly passed through the abdominal wall, then through the upper wall of the neobladder, and finally positioned into the ureters.

The surgeon fixes the urethral catheters to the urethra using a transfixion suture. A direct anastomosis is then performed with interrupted stitches. The upper portion of the neobladder is closed with a continuous suture, followed by a leak test to ensure integrity.
